questions.question: Why do data center standards exist?

choices.text: So every data center uses the same brand of server rack.
So offices can avoid following wiring and fire safety rules.
So Tier IV can be built without extra cost.
So cloud software can replace physical security systems.
So data centers are built safely, consistently, and reliably.

questions.explanation: The standards are official guidelines intended to make construction safe, consistent, and reliable.

questions.question: Data center standards are compared to which familiar concept?

choices.text: Building codes for a house, including wiring, plumbing, and fire safety rules.
Driver manuals for choosing the fastest route to a house.
Restaurant menus that define customer service options.
Hospital patient charts that list server temperatures.
Office dress codes for staff entering secure rooms.

questions.explanation: The standards are compared to building codes, but stricter and specialized for data centers.

questions.question: What does TIA stand for in TIA-942?

choices.text: Technical Infrastructure Authority.
Telecommunications Industry Association.
Tiered Internet Architecture.
Thermal Inspection Agency.
Telecom Information Alliance.

questions.explanation: TIA stands for Telecommunications Industry Association.

questions.question: How is TIA-942 characterized?

choices.text: As the climate rulebook for exact temperature and humidity.
As the fire code for sprinkler design only.
As the master blueprint for designing a data center.
As the software dashboard that monitors infrastructure.
As the disaster recovery metric for acceptable data loss.

questions.explanation: TIA-942 is described as the master blueprint for data center design.

questions.question: Which concern is specifically included under TIA-942 room layout and spaces?

choices.text: How much recent data can be lost after a disaster.
Whether smoke particles are detected by a laser.
Which visitors should be escorted through a building.
Where to put servers, cooling units, and how to organize the floor.
How many minutes a UPS battery must power a rack.

questions.explanation: The room layout item covers server placement, cooling unit placement, and floor organization.

questions.question: Which TIA-942 coverage item deals with running network and power cables properly?

choices.text: Fire protection.
The Tier system.
Cooling systems.
Room layout and spaces.
Cabling.

questions.explanation: The TIA-942 list has a separate cabling item for properly running network and power cables.

questions.question: Which TIA-942 topic focuses on wiring the building for maximum reliability?

choices.text: Electrical power design.
Cooling systems.
Fire protection.
Room layout and spaces.
The Tier system only.

questions.explanation: Electrical power design is the TIA-942 item about wiring the building for maximum reliability.

questions.question: Under TIA-942, what is the purpose of the cooling systems topic?

choices.text: To define exact RPO values for replication.
To keep servers at safe temperatures.
To detect smoke particles before visible flame.
To route visitors through mantrap entry rooms.
To classify NFPA sprinkler requirements.

questions.explanation: TIA-942 includes cooling systems as a design topic for keeping servers at safe temperatures.

questions.question: What does TIA-942 fire protection cover?

choices.text: How to calculate yearly downtime from uptime percentages.
How to place cold sites at a recommended distance.
How to detect and suppress fires without damaging equipment.
How to decide between single-phase and three-phase power.
How to keep humidity between office comfort ranges.

questions.explanation: The TIA-942 list includes fire protection for detecting and suppressing fires without equipment damage.

questions.question: What does the Tier system inside TIA-942 rank?

choices.text: How humid the server room is, from dry to wet.
How quickly VESDA can detect smoke in pipes.
How expensive each rack is, from low to high.
How reliable the data center is, from Tier I to Tier IV.
How far the DR site is from the primary site.

questions.explanation: The Tier system is described as a ranking from Tier I to Tier IV that indicates reliability.

questions.question: What does ASHRAE stand for?

choices.text: Association for Secure Hosting, Routing, and Electrical Engineering.
American Standard for Hardware Reliability and Equipment Efficiency.
Automated System for Humidity, Refrigeration, and Airflow Evaluation.
Applied Server Heating Regulation and Energy Authority.
American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers.

questions.explanation: ASHRAE stands for American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers.

questions.question: What does ASHRAE TC 9.9 define for servers?

choices.text: The exact temperature and humidity that servers need.
The number of roads in the Tier analogy.
The rules for preventing and handling fires.
The wiring code for electrical panels.
The maximum distance to every disaster recovery site.

questions.explanation: ASHRAE TC 9.9 is described as defining exact temperature and humidity requirements.

questions.question: How are ASHRAE recommended and allowable ranges distinguished?

choices.text: Recommended applies to power systems; allowable applies only to fire suppression agents.
Recommended is ideal for servers to thrive; allowable is the absolute limit before equipment starts failing.
Recommended is for offices; allowable is for data centers.
Recommended is the maximum legal distance to a DR site; allowable is the minimum.
Recommended is for cold aisles; allowable is only for hot aisles.

questions.explanation: The recommended range is ideal, while allowable range marks limits before equipment failure risk.

questions.question: In the ASHRAE doctor analogy, what is the main lesson?

choices.text: Servers must always run at the same temperature as human patients.
Humidity is irrelevant if temperature stays exactly between 36 and 37 degrees C.
Equipment may survive outside ideal conditions, but operating near the limits is risky.
ASHRAE controls hospital patient care, not data center climate.
Allowable ranges are safer than recommended ranges because they are wider.

questions.explanation: The analogy says a patient should be in an ideal range and can survive nearby values, but with risk; this maps to recommended versus allowable ranges.

questions.question: What does NFPA stand for?

choices.text: National Facility Power Authority.
Network Fire Prevention Alliance.
New Framework for Physical Access.
National Fire Protection Association.
Neutral Facility Protection Agency.

questions.explanation: NFPA stands for National Fire Protection Association.

questions.question: What general area do NFPA rules cover?

choices.text: Calculating PUE from power measurements.
Defining humidity and temperature ranges.
Ranking data centers by uptime tiers.
Configuring database log replication.
Preventing and handling fires.

questions.explanation: NFPA writes the rules for preventing and handling fires.

questions.question: Which NFPA standard is identified as protecting IT equipment from fire?

choices.text: NFPA 75.
NFPA 76.
NFPA 70 (NEC).
NFPA 13.

questions.explanation: The table maps NFPA 75 to protecting IT equipment from fire.

questions.question: Which standard covers protecting telecommunications facilities?

choices.text: NFPA 75.
NFPA 76.
NFPA 70 (NEC).
NFPA 13.
ASHRAE TC 9.9.

questions.explanation: The table maps NFPA 76 to telecommunications facility protection.

questions.question: Which standard is the National Electrical Code for electrical wiring safety?

choices.text: NFPA 75.
NFPA 76.
NFPA 70 (NEC).
NFPA 13.
TIA-942 Tier IV.

questions.explanation: NFPA 70 (NEC) is listed as electrical wiring safety and the National Electrical Code.

questions.question: Which NFPA standard covers sprinkler system design?

choices.text: NFPA 75.
NFPA 76.
NFPA 70 (NEC).
NFPA 13.
ASHRAE TC 9.9.

questions.explanation: The table lists NFPA 13 as the standard for sprinkler system design.

questions.question: Who created the Tiers ranking system?

choices.text: The National Fire Protection Association.
ASHRAE TC 9.9.
The Telecommunications Industry Association.
A Network Operations Center team.
The Uptime Institute.

questions.explanation: The Uptime Institute created the Tiers ranking system.

questions.question: What does the Tier system classify?

choices.text: Data centers by reliability.
Fire agents by environmental impact.
Servers by processor speed.
Racks by weight capacity.
Replication methods by file type only.

questions.explanation: The Tiers classify data centers by reliability.

questions.question: Which statement matches Tier I in the tier table?

choices.text: Redundant Components, 99.741% uptime, about 22.7 hours of downtime, multiple active paths.
Basic, 99.671% uptime, about 28.8 hours of downtime per year, single power and cooling path with no backup.
Concurrently Maintainable, 99.982% uptime, about 1.6 hours of downtime, automatic fault survival.
Fault Tolerant, 99.995% uptime, about 26 minutes of downtime, no backup components.
Basic, 99.995% uptime, about 26 minutes of downtime, two active power paths.

questions.explanation: Tier I is Basic with 99.671% uptime, about 28.8 hours downtime, a single path, and no backup.

questions.question: Which statement best describes Tier II?

choices.text: It has multiple power and cooling paths that allow maintenance without shutdown.
It has automatic fault survival even if a component fails.
It has backup components such as an extra UPS, but still only one path.
It has no backup components and a single path for both power and cooling.
It is mainly defined by a maximum practical DR distance of 300 km.

questions.explanation: Tier II improves on Tier I by adding redundant components while still retaining only one path.

questions.question: What makes Tier III concurrently maintainable?

choices.text: One power path is enough because the UPS runs for days.
The system automatically survives every component failure without design trade-offs.
It uses only backup software, not additional physical paths.
Multiple power and cooling paths allow repair or replacement without shutting anything down.
It removes the need for planned maintenance entirely.

questions.explanation: Tier III is defined by multiple paths and the ability to maintain equipment without shutdown.

questions.question: Which feature distinguishes Tier IV from Tier III in the table?

choices.text: Tier IV removes multiple paths and relies on a single reliable path.
Tier IV has about 1.6 hours of downtime per year instead of minutes.
Tier IV adds backup components but remains a single-path design.
Tier IV is intended only for small company blogs.
Tier IV adds automatic survival of component failures, so nothing goes down when a component fails.

questions.explanation: Tier IV includes everything in Tier III plus fault tolerance that automatically survives failures.

questions.question: Which tier has 99.982% uptime and about 1.6 hours of downtime per year?

choices.text: Tier III.
Tier I.
Tier II.
Tier IV.
Not a recognized tier.

questions.explanation: The Tier III row lists 99.982% uptime and about 1.6 hours of downtime per year.

questions.question: Which tier is associated with about 26 minutes of downtime per year?

choices.text: Tier I.
Tier IV.
Tier II.
Tier III.
A cold DR site.

questions.explanation: The Tier IV row lists about 26 minutes of yearly downtime.

questions.question: Which uptime and downtime pair belongs to Tier II?

choices.text: 99.671% uptime and about 28.8 hours of downtime per year.
99.982% uptime and about 1.6 hours of downtime per year.
99.741% uptime and about 22.7 hours of downtime per year.
99.995% uptime and about 26 minutes of downtime per year.
99.999% uptime and zero downtime per year.

questions.explanation: Tier II is listed as 99.741% uptime with about 22.7 hours of downtime per year.

questions.question: In the road analogy, what represents Tier I?

choices.text: A house with two roads and automatic route switching.
A house with the same road but a spare tire in the car.
A house with two roads so one can be used while the other is blocked.
A house with one road leading to it; if the road is blocked, you cannot get home.
A fully stocked second home ready for immediate use.

questions.explanation: Tier I is compared to a house with one road; a blockage prevents access.

questions.question: What does the spare tire represent in the Tier II analogy?

choices.text: Automatic fault tolerance across two roads.
A complete second data center that takes over immediately.
No backup components and no maintenance option.
A temperature range defined by ASHRAE.
Backup components while still having only the same single road/path.

questions.explanation: Tier II is still the same road, but with a spare tire, matching backup components on a single path.

questions.question: In the analogy, what does Tier III add?

choices.text: Two roads, so if one is blocked you take the other without stopping.
One road and no spare tire.
One road plus a spare tire only.
Two roads with automatic route switching before you notice a problem.
An empty apartment that must be furnished before use.

questions.explanation: Tier III is represented by two roads, matching multiple paths and maintainability.

questions.question: In the analogy, what does Tier IV represent?

choices.text: One road with no spare tire.
Two roads and automatic route switching if a problem is detected ahead.
One road with a spare tire.
Two roads but manual switching after a shutdown.
A single bridge that must be closed for maintenance.

questions.explanation: Tier IV adds automatic switching, matching fault tolerance.

questions.question: What is the stated cost and downtime trade-off for higher tiers?

choices.text: Higher tiers cost less because they use fewer redundant paths.
Higher tiers always eliminate the need for management processes.
Higher tiers cost more to build but provide dramatically less downtime.
Higher tiers are cheaper but require more yearly downtime.
Higher tiers mainly improve humidity, not availability.

questions.explanation: The key takeaway explicitly says higher tiers cost more but reduce downtime dramatically.

questions.question: Which organization type is said to typically need Tier III or IV?

choices.text: Small company blogs.
Personal laptop backups.
Temporary office printer rooms.
Banks and hospitals.
Offline document archives.

questions.explanation: Banks and hospitals typically need Tier III or IV, while a small company blog might be fine with Tier I.

questions.question: What tier might be sufficient for a small company blog?

choices.text: Tier II.
Tier III.
Tier IV.
A hot site.
Tier I.

questions.explanation: The guide contrast banks and hospitals with a small company blog that might be fine with Tier I.

questions.question: What percentage of downtime is attributed to planned maintenance?
questions.explanation: The downtime table lists planned maintenance at 30%.

questions.question: What percentage of downtime is attributed to server software issues?
questions.explanation: Server software issues are listed at 30% in the downtime table.

questions.question: How much downtime is attributed to human error?
questions.explanation: Human error is listed as 15% of downtime causes.

questions.question: What percentage of downtime is attributed to hardware failure?
questions.explanation: Hardware failure is listed as 10%.

questions.question: Which set contains only causes that are each listed as 5% of downtime?

choices.text: Planned maintenance, server software issues, and human error.
Human error, hardware failure, and network software.
Server software issues, hardware failure, and environmental issues.
Planned maintenance, client software, and hardware failure.
Network software, client software, and environmental issues.

questions.explanation: The 5% rows are network software, client software, and environmental issues such as overheating.

questions.question: What accounts for 60% of downtime?

choices.text: Planned work and software problems.
Hardware failure and environmental overheating.
Human error and physical security failures.
Cooling problems and power grid failures only.
Client software and network cables only.

questions.explanation: The guide highlight that planned work and software problems together account for 60% of downtime.

questions.question: What conclusion is drawn from the downtime causes?

choices.text: Physical equipment breaking is the only serious outage cause.
Good management and processes matter as much as good hardware.
Hardware quality makes software processes irrelevant.
Environmental problems always dominate downtime statistics.
The tier system eliminates the need for maintenance planning.

questions.explanation: Because many outages come from planned work and software, the guide emphasize management and processes.

questions.question: Which high availability pillar means components are built to work consistently without failure?

choices.text: Resiliency.
Availability.
Reliability.
Serviceability.
Maintainability tiering.

questions.explanation: Reliability is defined as components working consistently without failure.

questions.question: Which pillar means the system can recover quickly when something fails?

choices.text: Reliability.
Availability.
Serviceability.
Resiliency.
Humidity control.

questions.explanation: Resiliency is the ability to recover quickly after a failure.

questions.question: Which pillar means services remain accessible to users as much as possible?

choices.text: Reliability.
Resiliency.
Serviceability.
Power density.
Availability.

questions.explanation: Availability is defined as keeping services accessible to users as much as possible.

questions.question: Which pillar means equipment can be maintained or replaced without disrupting operations?

choices.text: Serviceability.
Reliability.
Resiliency.
Availability.
Static transfer.

questions.explanation: Serviceability is the ability to maintain or replace equipment without disrupting operations.

questions.question: What would you see when walking into the described data center interior?

choices.text: Only desks, office chairs, and desktop monitors for employees.
Long rows of tall metal cabinets called racks standing on a smooth floor.
A single generator room with no server equipment.
Rows of water tanks used to submerge all servers.
Mostly public counters where customers access bank websites.

questions.explanation: The guide describe long rows of tall metal cabinets called racks.

questions.question: What is the purpose of server racks or cabinets?

choices.text: They are pipes that sample air for smoke particles.
They are final electrical panels inside each server.
They are tall metal frames that hold servers, like bookshelves for computers.
They are software dashboards for monitoring infrastructure.
They are rooms with two doors that prevent tailgating.

questions.explanation: Server racks/cabinets are the frames that hold stacked servers.

questions.question: What do cable trays and ladder racks do?

choices.text: They seal the hot aisle and route air to cooling units.
They distribute high-voltage power to each rack.
They detect smoke before a visible flame appears.
They organize thousands of network and power cables overhead.
They calculate total facility power divided by IT power.

questions.explanation: Cable trays and ladder racks are overhead pathways for organizing network and power cables.

questions.question: What role do aisles play in the physical data center layout?

choices.text: They are battery strings that run servers for 5 to 30 minutes.
They are gas agents that leave no residue after fire suppression.
They are official standards for sprinkler system design.
They are transaction logs copied between databases.
They are walkways between rack rows, carefully designed for airflow.

questions.explanation: Aisles are walkways between racks and are designed with airflow in mind.

questions.question: Why are ramps listed as a key physical element?

choices.text: They help move heavy equipment in and out safely.
They allow cold air to rise through perforated tiles.
They switch power from the utility to a generator.
They keep visitors from entering behind authorized staff.
They reduce network latency between DR sites.

questions.explanation: Ramps are included for safe movement of heavy equipment.

questions.question: What is the structural floor responsible for?

choices.text: It is the upper removable tile layer that always provides airflow.
It is the actual concrete floor and must support tons of equipment.
It is the chilled water system that cools CRAH units.
It is the external road that represents Tier I reliability.
It is the software layer that tracks rack space in DCIM.

questions.explanation: The structural floor is the actual building floor, strong enough for heavy equipment.

questions.question: How high above the concrete floor is the raised floor usually built?

choices.text: About 5 to 30 cm.
About 120 to 160 km.
About 30 to 90 cm.
About 18 to 27 cm.
About 5 to 30 minutes.

questions.explanation: Most raised floors are about 30 to 90 cm above the actual concrete floor.

questions.question: What supports the raised floor?

choices.text: Generator coils.
VESDA sampling pipes.
Biometric scanners.
Adjustable metal pedestals.
Power panels.

questions.explanation: The raised floor is supported by adjustable metal pedestals.

questions.question: What is the plenum in the raised floor design?

choices.text: The hot aisle sealed by containment panels.
The room where NOC staff watch wall screens.
The backup site that takes over after a disaster.
The outer fence and gate area around a building.
The useful space between the raised floor and the concrete floor.

questions.explanation: The plenum is the space between the real concrete floor and raised floor.

questions.question: How does the raised floor help deliver cold air?

choices.text: Cold air is pushed into the plenum and rises through perforated tiles where servers need it.
Hot air is pushed into the plenum and stored there until maintenance.
Cold air is sent only through solid tiles in walkways.
The plenum contains generator coils that create chilled water.
The plenum seals the hot aisle and routes smoke to VESDA.

questions.explanation: The guide explain that cold air moves through the plenum and up through perforated tiles.

questions.question: How can the raised floor help cable management?

choices.text: It removes the need for any overhead cable trays.
Power and network cables can run underneath, keeping the main floor organized.
It converts mechanical power into electrical power.
It forces all cables through visitor management logs.
It copies database transaction logs to a DR site.

questions.explanation: Cable routing under the raised floor keeps the main floor clean and organized.

questions.question: What grounding benefit does the raised floor framework provide?

choices.text: It provides network grounding that lowers internet latency.
It provides fire grounding that replaces clean agents.
It provides electrical grounding that protects equipment against static electricity.
It provides software grounding that prevents database lag.
It provides access grounding that stops tailgating.

questions.explanation: The metal framework provides grounding to protect against static electricity.

questions.question: Where are solid floor tiles used?

choices.text: Only in cold aisles where servers need direct cooling.
Only under generator coils to convert mechanical energy.
Only in mantraps to prevent tailgating.
Where airflow is not wanted, such as walkways.
Only inside CRAC refrigerant lines.

questions.explanation: Solid tiles are used where airflow is not desired, with walkways given as an example.

questions.question: What is the purpose of perforated tiles?

choices.text: They block airflow in walkways to prevent overcooling.
They act as batteries while generators start.
They seal the hot aisle and route smoke to the NOC.
They detect motion and trigger door alarms.
Their holes let cold air flow up from the plenum into server aisles.

questions.explanation: Perforated tiles allow cold air to rise from the plenum into server aisles.

questions.question: Why is power described as the most critical data center system?

choices.text: If power is lost, servers shut down, websites go offline, and transactions fail.
Because power systems directly reduce humidity without cooling equipment.
Because power systems replace all monitoring and security staff.
Because power systems determine the distance to the DR site.
Because power systems are only needed during planned maintenance.

questions.explanation: Everything stops if the data center loses power.

questions.question: Why do data centers use multiple layers of power protection?

choices.text: Each layer removes the need for cooling in the server room.
Each layer backs up the one before it to keep equipment running.
Each layer converts hot aisle air into chilled water.
Each layer increases downtime so maintenance is easier.
Each layer is mainly for visitor authentication.

questions.explanation: The guide explain that multiple protection layers back each other up.

questions.question: Which sequence matches the power chain from outside to server?

choices.text: Utility Power -> UPS -> ATS/STS -> Power Panels -> PDUs -> Individual Servers.
Generator -> PDUs -> ATS/STS -> UPS -> Power Panels -> Utility Power.
Utility Power -> ATS/STS -> UPS -> PDUs -> Power Panels -> Individual Servers.
Utility Power -> Power Panels -> PDUs -> UPS -> ATS/STS -> Individual Servers.
UPS -> Utility Power -> PDUs -> Generator Coil -> CRAC -> Individual Servers.

questions.explanation: The diagram lists the order as utility power, transfer switch, UPS, PDUs, power panels, and servers.

questions.question: What is utility power in the data center power chain?

choices.text: Stored battery power that bridges generator startup time.
A diesel engine that independently generates electricity for days.
The last connection point inside each server rack.
Regular electricity from the power company, used as the primary source but not guaranteed.
The electronic switch that changes sources in milliseconds.

questions.explanation: Utility power is normal grid electricity from the power company, but storms and failures can interrupt it.

questions.question: What does an ATS do?

choices.text: It electronically switches in milliseconds with no moving parts.
It stores battery power for 5 to 30 minutes.
It distributes high-voltage power to each individual rack.
It converts mechanical power into electricity using a coil.
It detects utility power failure and automatically switches to the backup generator.

questions.explanation: An Automatic Transfer Switch detects utility failure and switches to generator power.

questions.question: What is the key distinction of an STS compared with an ATS?

choices.text: It switches electronically with no moving parts, in milliseconds.
It is a battery bridge that runs equipment for 5 to 30 minutes.
It converts diesel fuel into mechanical power for days.
It is the final connection point inside each server rack.
It monitors each rack power draw like an industrial power strip.

questions.explanation: A Static Transfer Switch performs the switching electronically and very quickly.

questions.question: What critical gap does the UPS fill?

choices.text: The gap between hot aisle containment and cold aisle containment.
The few seconds between utility power going out and the generator starting up.
The network latency gap when DR sites are more than 300 km apart.
The humidity gap between recommended and allowable ASHRAE ranges.
The visitor gap between the first and second mantrap doors.

questions.explanation: The UPS answers what happens in the seconds before the generator starts.

questions.question: How long does the UPS typically keep systems running?

choices.text: 5 to 30 seconds.
30 to 90 minutes.
5 to 30 minutes.
40 to 50 minutes.
120 to 160 minutes.

questions.explanation: The UPS keeps everything running for 5 to 30 minutes.

questions.question: What is the role of a power generator?

choices.text: A battery system provides only a few milliseconds of power.
A software dashboard tracks rack space and cable connections.
A switch detects smoke particles in sampled air.
A diesel or gas-powered engine can generate electricity independently for hours or days.
A chiller plant uses refrigerant inside each rack.

questions.explanation: The generator is an engine-driven backup source that can run for hours or days.

questions.question: Which pair of parts is listed for a power generator?

choices.text: A battery string and a perforated tile that delivers cold air.
A VESDA pipe and a laser that converts smoke into power.
A mantrap door and an access card reader that produce electricity.
A chiller plant and a refrigerant loop that create static pressure.
An engine that produces mechanical power and a generator coil that converts it into electricity.

questions.explanation: The generator has an engine for mechanical power and a coil that converts mechanical power into electricity.

questions.question: What do PDUs do in the power system?

choices.text: They distribute incoming high-voltage power safely to individual racks and monitor rack power use.
They switch utility power to generators in milliseconds.
They provide battery power while generators start.
They are the final connection points to individual servers.
They generate electricity independently for days.

questions.explanation: Power Distribution Units distribute power to racks and monitor how much power each rack uses.

questions.question: What are power panels?

choices.text: Transfer switches that move from grid power to generator power.
Final connection points that deliver power to individual servers within each rack.
Massive batteries that bridge a generator startup delay.
Software dashboards that track cooling efficiency.
Fire suppression agents that leave no residue.

questions.explanation: Power panels are the last-mile connection points to individual servers.

questions.question: How is single-phase power described?

choices.text: Industrial-strength electricity used by most data center equipment.
Battery backup power that runs for days.
Standard household electricity used for lighter equipment.
A transfer switch with no moving parts.
Power delivered through chilled water.

questions.explanation: Single-phase is described as standard household electricity for lighter equipment.

questions.question: How is three-phase power described?

choices.text: Standard household electricity used mostly for lighter equipment.
Battery power that fills only the first few seconds of an outage.
A static switch that electronically selects between sources.
Industrial-strength electricity that delivers more power more efficiently; most data center equipment uses it.
The final connection inside each individual server.

questions.explanation: Three-phase power is industrial-strength, more efficient, and commonly used by data center equipment.

questions.question: Which summary matches the minimum power backup layers?

choices.text: Utility power -> perforated tiles -> CRAC units.
Generator -> DCIM -> visitor management.
UPS batteries -> VESDA -> clean agents.
Single-phase -> three-phase -> hot site.
Utility power -> UPS batteries -> generators.

questions.explanation: The power summary says data centers have at least utility power, UPS batteries, and generators.

questions.question: What does the power summary claim about a grid outage lasting days?

choices.text: The data center can stay on because UPS bridges to generators.
The data center must shut down because UPS batteries last only milliseconds.
The data center stays on only if the raised floor is 90 cm high.
The data center can stay on because CRAC uses refrigerant.
The data center cannot stay on unless it is a cold DR site.

questions.explanation: Even if the power grid goes down for days, the layered power system keeps the data center on.

questions.question: Why does cooling matter so much in a data center?

choices.text: Most servers stop generating heat when they are stacked in racks.
Thousands of tightly packed servers generate enormous heat.
Cooling is needed mainly to make visitors comfortable.
Cooling replaces the need for network connections.
Servers run only during short backup windows.

questions.explanation: The heat from thousands of servers running in tightly packed racks.

questions.question: A single rack can generate about as much heat as what?

choices.text: 1 to 2 laptop chargers running simultaneously.
30 to 90 raised-floor pedestals.
10 to 20 space heaters running simultaneously.
40 to 50 km of network fiber.
5 to 30 UPS batteries in standby mode.

questions.explanation: The source compares a single rack heat output to 10 to 20 space heaters.

questions.question: What can servers do when they overheat?

choices.text: They automatically become Tier IV fault tolerant.
They produce less heat by increasing processor speed.
They switch from CRAC to CRAH by themselves.
They shut down to protect themselves.
They convert humidity into backup power.

questions.explanation: Without cooling, servers can overheat and shut down for self-protection.

questions.question: Why can performance degrade without cooling?

choices.text: Processors speed up to finish work before shutdown.
PDUs stop monitoring rack power use.
Network connections become physically shorter.
Clean agents leave residue on chips.
Processors slow down to reduce heat.

questions.explanation: Processors slow down to reduce heat, causing performance degradation.

questions.question: What is the number one enemy of electronics?

choices.text: Heat.
Cold air.
Access cards.
Fiber cabling.
Database logs.

questions.explanation: Hardware lifespan shrinks because heat is the number one enemy of electronics.

questions.question: What business impact can cooling failure cause?

choices.text: The PUE becomes exactly 1.0 automatically.
Critical services can go offline, affecting millions of users.
The DR site becomes a hot site immediately.
Human error is eliminated from downtime causes.
The data center needs fewer security layers.

questions.explanation: Cooling failure can make critical services go offline and affect many users.

questions.question: What recommended server temperature range is listed from ASHRAE TC 9.9?

choices.text: 15 degrees C maximum only.
18 degrees C to 27 degrees C (64 degrees F to 81 degrees F).
30 degrees C to 90 degrees C.
10 degrees C to 20 degrees C.

questions.explanation: The cooling table lists 18 to 27 degrees C, or 64 to 81 degrees F.

questions.question: What recommended relative humidity range is listed?

choices.text: 15% maximum.

questions.explanation: The table lists relative humidity at 40% to 60%.

questions.question: What maximum dew point is listed?

choices.text: 18 degrees C (64 degrees F) maximum.
27 degrees C (81 degrees F) maximum.
40 degrees C (60 degrees F) maximum.
30 degrees C (90 degrees F) maximum.
15 degrees C (59 degrees F) maximum.

questions.explanation: The table lists a dew point of 15 degrees C or 59 degrees F maximum.

questions.question: Why is air that is too dry dangerous for servers?

choices.text: Static electricity can build up and fry circuits.
Moisture condenses on equipment and causes short circuits.
Generator coils stop converting mechanical power.
Network latency becomes too high for replication.
VESDA cannot sample air through pipes.

questions.explanation: Too dry conditions increase static electricity that can damage circuits.

questions.question: Why is air that is too humid dangerous for servers?

choices.text: Static electricity builds up and fries circuits.
Moisture can condense on equipment and cause short circuits.
The UPS can run only for milliseconds.
A hot aisle becomes sealed by itself.
PDUs stop distributing high-voltage power.

questions.explanation: Too much humidity can produce condensation and short circuits.

questions.question: Why do data centers use Precision Air Conditioning rather than regular AC?

choices.text: Regular AC removes no moisture and focuses entirely on server racks.
PAC is used mainly to make people comfortable in office spaces.
PAC is designed for server rooms and focuses almost entirely on removing heat.
PAC replaces all humidity management with fire suppression gas.
Regular AC is required for all Tier IV facilities by NFPA 13.

questions.explanation: Regular AC is designed for people and moisture removal; PAC is specialized for servers and heat removal.

questions.question: What is regular air conditioning primarily designed for according to the comparison?

choices.text: Keeping servers comfortable by focusing only on heat removal.
Routing hot air directly to cooling units in a sealed aisle.
Maintaining positive pressure to keep dust out of all buildings.
Keeping people comfortable, while removing a lot of moisture.
Delivering chilled water from a central plant to CRAH units.

questions.explanation: The guide contrast regular AC for people and moisture removal with PAC for servers and heat.

questions.question: What does High Sensible Heat Ratio (SHR) mean in this context?

choices.text: PAC seals the hot aisle while leaving the cold aisle open.
PAC replicates data synchronously across two sites.
PAC converts utility power into battery backup.
PAC detects smoke before a visible flame appears.
PAC removes heat without over-drying the air.

questions.explanation: SHR is listed as a reason PAC is used because it removes heat without over-drying.

questions.question: Which PAC feature keeps humidity levels precise?

choices.text: Humidity management.
Static transfer switching.
Generator coil conversion.
Water mist suppression.
Tailgating prevention.

questions.explanation: Humidity management is one of the technical reasons PAC is used.

questions.question: Why does PAC maintain positive air pressure inside the data center?

choices.text: To reduce RPO to zero.
To keep dust out.
To make water mist evaporate faster.
To switch utility power in milliseconds.
To make server software issues less common.

questions.explanation: PAC air pressure control maintains positive pressure to keep dust out.

questions.question: How does a CRAC cool the room?

choices.text: It uses chilled water from a central chiller plant.
It places small cooling units directly between racks.
It uses refrigerant to absorb heat from room air and dump it outside.
It runs liquid pipes directly through server hardware.
It seals the cold aisle so air only reaches intakes.

questions.explanation: CRAC works like a larger traditional AC and uses refrigerant.

questions.question: How does a CRAH cool air?

choices.text: It uses refrigerant inside a self-contained unit.
It sprays clean agent gas to cool server chips.
It stores cold air in UPS batteries.
It uses chilled water from a central chiller plant, usually outside the building.
It uses visitor management logs to control airflow.

questions.explanation: CRAH uses chilled water from a central chiller plant instead of refrigerant.

questions.question: Which comparison is correct for CRAC and CRAH energy efficiency?

choices.text: CRAC is higher efficiency; CRAH is lower efficiency.
Both have identical efficiency but different fire agents.
CRAC efficiency is measured only by RPO; CRAH by RTO.
CRAC is efficient only for hot sites; CRAH only for cold sites.
CRAC is lower efficiency; CRAH is higher efficiency.

questions.explanation: The table lists CRAC energy efficiency as lower and CRAH as higher.

questions.question: Which installation cost comparison is listed?

choices.text: CRAC has lower installation cost; CRAH has higher installation cost.
CRAC has higher installation cost; CRAH has lower installation cost.
Both have no installation cost because they are software systems.
CRAC cost is lowest only in large data centers; CRAH is lowest only in small rooms.
Installation cost is not compared.

questions.explanation: The table lists CRAC installation cost as lower and CRAH as higher.

questions.question: Which operating cost comparison is listed?

choices.text: CRAC has lower operating cost; CRAH has higher operating cost.
CRAC has higher operating cost; CRAH has lower operating cost.
Both have no operating cost after installation.
CRAC operating cost is measured in downtime hours.
CRAH operating cost is higher because it is less efficient.

questions.explanation: The table says CRAC operating cost is higher and CRAH operating cost is lower.

questions.question: Which maintenance comparison is correct?

choices.text: CRAC maintenance is more complex; CRAH maintenance is simpler.
Both require no maintenance because they run automatically.
CRAC maintenance is simpler; CRAH maintenance is more complex.
CRAC requires database log maintenance; CRAH requires rack lock maintenance.
Maintenance is identical because both use refrigerant.

questions.explanation: CRAC is listed as simpler to maintain, while CRAH is more complex.

questions.question: Which best-for comparison is listed?

choices.text: CRAC is best for large data centers; CRAH is best for small to medium rooms.
CRAC is best for disaster recovery only; CRAH is best for mantraps only.
CRAC is best for extinguishing fires; CRAH is best for detecting smoke.
CRAC is best for small to medium rooms; CRAH is best for large data centers.
Both are listed only for single racks, not rooms.

questions.explanation: The table says CRAC fits small to medium rooms and CRAH fits large data centers.

questions.question: What is a memory aid for CRAC?

choices.text: CRAC is part of a larger system, like central air in a big building.
CRAC is a gas that leaves no residue.
CRAC is a room with two locked doors.
CRAC is a dashboard for infrastructure management.
CRAC is a self-contained unit, like a window AC.

questions.explanation: CRAC as a self-contained unit like window AC.

questions.question: What is a memory aid for CRAH?

choices.text: CRAH is part of a larger system, like central air in a big building.
CRAH is a self-contained unit, like a window AC.
CRAH is an automatic power transfer switch.
CRAH is a clean agent that absorbs fire heat.
CRAH is a database log replication layer.

questions.explanation: CRAH is part of a larger system, comparable to central air.

questions.question: What is in-row cooling?

choices.text: Cold air delivered only through underfloor perforated tiles.
Small cooling units placed directly between server racks for targeted cooling.
Pipes carrying liquid directly through server hardware.
A sealed hot aisle routed back to cooling units.
A refrigerant-based room air conditioner outside the building.

questions.explanation: In-row cooling places units directly between racks and cools exactly where heat is generated.

questions.question: Which statement matches liquid cooling?

choices.text: Small air units are placed between racks to cool the whole room.
Refrigerant absorbs heat from room air and dumps it outside.
Cool liquid is carried directly to or through server hardware; it is the most efficient method for powerful servers.
Chilled water cools air inside an air handler but never reaches hardware.
Cold air is trapped in an enclosed aisle and only reaches intakes.

questions.explanation: Liquid cooling carries cool liquid directly to or through server hardware and is used for powerful servers.

questions.question: How are racks arranged in the common hot aisle/cold aisle layout?

choices.text: Racks are scattered randomly so hot and cold air mix evenly.
All rack backs face cold aisles and all fronts face hot aisles.
Only one rack row is used so containment is unnecessary.
Racks are arranged in rows, all facing the same direction.
Racks are arranged around a mantrap entry room.

questions.explanation: The airflow sequence begins with server racks in rows all facing the same direction.

questions.question: Which side of each server faces the cold aisle?

choices.text: The back, where it exhausts hot air.
The top, where generator coils sit.
The side, where power panels are installed.
The bottom, where VESDA pipes sample air.
The front, where it sucks in cool air.

questions.explanation: The front of each server faces the cold aisle because it draws in cool air.

questions.question: Which side of each server faces the hot aisle?

choices.text: The back, where it blows out hot air.
The front, where it sucks in cool air.
The bottom, where battery power enters.
The top, where visitor logs are stored.
The side, where clean agents are sprayed.

questions.explanation: The back of each server faces the hot aisle because it exhausts hot air.

questions.question: Where is cold air delivered through perforated floor tiles?

choices.text: Into the hot aisle.
Into the cold aisle.
Into the NOC room only.
Into the mantrap between two doors.
Into the generator coil.

questions.explanation: The airflow steps say cold air is delivered through perforated tiles into the cold aisle.

questions.question: After servers exhaust hot air into the hot aisle, what happens next?

choices.text: The hot air is stored in UPS batteries until the generator starts.
The hot air is sent directly to the DR site for replication.
The hot air rises, is captured by cooling systems, cooled, and pushed back under the floor.
The hot air is sealed forever inside solid tiles.
The hot air triggers biometric authentication at sensitive areas.

questions.explanation: The guide describe the hot air rising, being captured, cooled, and returned under the floor.

questions.question: Why is containment used?

choices.text: To keep visitors from tailgating through access doors.
To keep utility power from switching to generators.
To keep data replication from becoming synchronous.
To keep hot and cold air from mixing and wasting energy.
To keep sprinkler water away from clean agents.

questions.explanation: The containment section says mixed hot and cold air wastes energy.

questions.question: What is enclosed in Hot Aisle Containment (HAC)?

choices.text: The cold aisle is sealed.
Both aisles are left open.
Only the raised floor plenum is sealed.
Only the NOC room is sealed.
The hot aisle is sealed.

questions.explanation: The comparison table says HAC seals the hot aisle.

questions.question: What is enclosed in Cold Aisle Containment (CAC)?

choices.text: The cold aisle is sealed.
The hot aisle is sealed.
The generator room is sealed.
The utility power feed is sealed.
The DR site is sealed.

questions.explanation: The comparison table says CAC seals the cold aisle.

questions.question: How does HAC work?

choices.text: It traps cold air so it only goes to server intakes.
It traps hot air and routes it directly back to cooling units.
It pulls smoke through pipes for laser analysis.
It runs chilled water directly through server processors.
It switches power sources without moving parts.

questions.explanation: HAC traps hot air and routes it directly to cooling units.

questions.question: How does CAC work?

choices.text: It traps hot air and routes it directly to cooling units.
It sprays a clean agent without leaving residue.
It traps cold air so it only goes to server intakes.
It distributes high-voltage power to racks.
It copies raw storage blocks to a second site.

questions.explanation: CAC traps cold air so it is delivered to server intakes.

questions.question: Which cooling efficiency comparison is listed?

choices.text: HAC is high; CAC is very high.
Both are low because they mix hot and cold air.
HAC is lower because it is simpler.
HAC is very high; CAC is high.
CAC is lower because it is used only in hot sites.

questions.explanation: The table lists HAC cooling efficiency as very high and CAC as high.

questions.question: Which complexity comparison is listed?

choices.text: HAC is simpler; CAC is moderate.
Both are more complex than liquid cooling by definition.
HAC is impossible in large-scale centers.
CAC is complex because it uses chilled water.
HAC is moderate; CAC is simpler.

questions.explanation: The table lists HAC complexity as moderate and CAC as simpler.

questions.question: Which containment best-fit statement is correct?

choices.text: HAC is best for large-scale, high-density centers; CAC is best for most standard data centers.
HAC is best for most standard data centers; CAC is best for high-density centers.
HAC is only for offices; CAC is only for hospitals.
HAC is best for cold sites; CAC is best for hot sites.
HAC and CAC are not tied to data center scale.

questions.explanation: The table says HAC fits large-scale, high-density centers, while CAC fits most standard data centers.

questions.question: What does PUE stand for?

choices.text: Power Utility Efficiency.
Power Usage Effectiveness.
Physical Uptime Estimate.
Plenum Usage Effectiveness.
Primary Utility Engine.

questions.explanation: PUE is expanded as Power Usage Effectiveness.

questions.question: What does PUE measure?

choices.text: How fast a disaster recovery site can restore service.
How much data can be lost after a disaster.
How efficiently a data center uses its power.
How many components can fail in Tier IV.
How early smoke is detected before visible flame.

questions.explanation: PUE is described as a simple number indicating power-use efficiency.

questions.question: Which formula matches PUE?

choices.text: IT Equipment Power divided by Total Facility Power.
Cooling Power divided by Generator Runtime.
Downtime Hours divided by Uptime Percentage.
Total Facility Power divided by IT Equipment Power.
DR Distance divided by Replication Latency.

questions.explanation: The formula shown is PUE = Total Facility Power divided by IT Equipment Power.

questions.question: What does a PUE of 1.0 mean?

choices.text: Half of all power goes to cooling and other overhead.
The data center has about 1.0 hours of downtime per year.
The UPS runs for exactly 1 minute.
The DR site is 1 km away.
Perfect efficiency: every watt goes to servers and nothing is wasted.

questions.explanation: PUE 1.0 is perfect because all power goes to servers.

questions.question: What does a PUE of 2.0 indicate?

choices.text: Half the power goes to cooling, lighting, and other overhead.
Every watt goes directly to servers.
The data center has Tier II reliability.
Only 0.2 watts of overhead are used per server watt.
The facility is automatically fault tolerant.

questions.explanation: A PUE of 2.0 means facility power is twice IT power, so half is overhead.

questions.question: What PUE range is described as good for a modern data center?

choices.text: 1.0 to 1.1 only.

questions.explanation: The guide describe a good modern PUE as 1.2 to 1.4.

questions.question: If total facility power is 10 MW and IT equipment power is 7 MW, what PUE is given?
questions.explanation: The example calculates 10 divided by 7 as 1.43.

questions.question: In the 10 MW total and 7 MW IT example, what does the guide say about overhead per server watt?

choices.text: An additional 1.43 watts is spent on servers only.
No extra power is spent outside servers.
An additional 7 watts is spent on every 10 watts of cooling.
An additional 0.43 watts is spent on cooling, lighting, and other systems.
An additional 2.0 watts is spent because half the power is overhead.

questions.explanation: The example explains that PUE 1.43 means 0.43 extra watts of overhead per server watt.

questions.question: Why is traditional water sprinkler fire suppression a challenge in data centers?

choices.text: Water makes servers run too cold and increases PUE to 1.0.
Water prevents UPS batteries from bridging generator startup.
Water makes biometric authentication less accurate.
Water turns a cold site into a hot site.
Spraying water on servers could destroy them as surely as fire would.

questions.explanation: Water can destroy servers just like fire can.

questions.question: What fire detection requirement is listed for data centers?

choices.text: Detect fire extremely early, before flames even appear.
Detect fire only after sprinklers have discharged.
Detect fire only when server software logs an outage.
Detect fire after visitors report smoke at reception.
Detect fire by measuring PUE against IT power.

questions.explanation: Data centers must detect fire extremely early before flames appear.

questions.question: What fire suppression requirement is listed?

choices.text: Suppress fire by flooding all racks with water.
Suppress fire without damaging equipment.
Suppress fire by shutting down all servers permanently.
Suppress fire by opening cold aisle containment.
Suppress fire by increasing humidity above recommended range.

questions.explanation: The second requirement is to suppress fire without damaging equipment.

questions.question: What does VESDA stand for?

choices.text: Virtual Emergency Server Detection Agent.
Ventilated Equipment Smoke Distribution Array.
Very Early Smoke Detection Apparatus.
Voltage Event Safety Detection Alarm.
Verified Electronic Suppression Delivery Apparatus.

questions.explanation: VESDA is expanded as Very Early Smoke Detection Apparatus.

questions.question: How does VESDA detect smoke?

choices.text: It waits for smoke to drift up to a ceiling detector.
It measures PUE until cooling efficiency drops.
It monitors UPS battery levels and generator readiness.
It continuously samples air through pipes and analyzes it with a laser.
It scans visitor badges for tailgating attempts.

questions.explanation: VESDA is an air-sampling system that pulls air through pipes and uses a laser for analysis.

questions.question: What makes VESDA more proactive than a regular smoke detector?

choices.text: It activates only after flames are visible to a human.
It waits for smoke to drift upward to a fixed detector.
It detects only water leaks under raised floors.
It samples only hot aisle air after suppression starts.
It can detect smoke particles long before a human would notice, sometimes before visible flame.

questions.explanation: Very early detection, before human detection and sometimes before visible flame.

questions.question: How does the VESDA analogy contrast it with regular smoke detectors?

choices.text: Regular detectors wait for smoke to drift up; VESDA actively samples air everywhere all the time.
Regular detectors sample air everywhere; VESDA waits for smoke to drift up.
Regular detectors use lasers; VESDA uses only human inspection.
Regular detectors suppress fires; VESDA only measures humidity.
Regular detectors are for data centers; VESDA is for homes only.

questions.explanation: The analogy says VESDA actively sniffs air, unlike regular detectors that wait.

questions.question: What are clean agent fire suppression systems?

choices.text: Sprinkler systems that flood racks with water.
Gases that extinguish fire without leaving residue or damaging electronics.
Battery systems that bridge generator startup time.
Cooling systems that seal cold aisles.
Software tools that track cable connections.

questions.explanation: Clean agents are gases used to extinguish fire without residue or electronic damage.

questions.question: How does FM-200 work according to the table?

choices.text: It evaporates quickly and is more environmentally friendly than Novec 1230.
It sprays an ultra-fine mist that cools fire without soaking equipment.
It absorbs heat from the fire, lowering temperature below the point where combustion can continue.
It samples air through pipes and analyzes particles with a laser.
It removes static electricity by grounding raised floor pedestals.

questions.explanation: The FM-200 row says it absorbs heat and lowers the fire temperature below the combustion point.

questions.question: What distinguishes Novec 1230?

choices.text: It absorbs smoke with a laser before flames appear.
It sprays a flood of water over all equipment.
It is a refrigerant used by CRAC units to cool room air.
It is similar to FM-200 but more environmentally friendly, evaporates quickly, and leaves no residue.
It is a sprinkler design standard from NFPA.

questions.explanation: The table describes Novec 1230 as similar to FM-200, more environmentally friendly, quick to evaporate, and residue-free.

questions.question: How is water mist different from traditional water suppression?

choices.text: It floods server racks to remove heat as fast as possible.
It uses no water at all and works only as a gas.
It detects smoke particles before humans notice them.
It routes hot air directly back to cooling units.
It uses an ultra-fine mist that cools the fire without soaking equipment.

questions.explanation: Water mist is described as ultra-fine, not a flood, and cools without soaking equipment.

questions.question: Why is physical security as important as cybersecurity in data centers?

choices.text: Data centers hold some of the most valuable and sensitive information in the world.
Physical security directly lowers the PUE formula.
Cybersecurity is not relevant when servers are in racks.
Physical security replaces all need for fire protection.
Data centers hold only public information.

questions.explanation: The security section states that data centers hold valuable sensitive information, so physical security matters.

questions.question: What is the main idea of layered data center security?

choices.text: A single biometric scan is enough for all areas.
An attacker would have to get through every layer.
Only the outer fence matters if the network is fast.
Security layers are used only after a disaster.
CCTV replaces locked doors and access controls.

questions.explanation: The guide compare security to layers: an attacker must pass every layer.

questions.question: Which examples belong to perimeter security?

choices.text: Key cards and PIN codes inside locked doors.
Fingerprint, iris, or facial recognition.
Fences, gates, and guards around the building.
A two-door room that prevents tailgating.
Motion sensors and door sensors.

questions.explanation: Perimeter security is listed as fences, gates, and guards around the building.

questions.question: What is included in building access security?

choices.text: Fences, gates, and guards outside the building.
Fingerprint, iris, or facial recognition only at sensitive areas.
Cameras recording every angle at all times.
Locked doors with access control systems such as key cards and PIN codes.
Every non-employee being escorted and monitored.

questions.explanation: Building access uses locked doors with access control systems, including key cards and PIN codes.

questions.question: Which set contains examples of biometric authentication?

choices.text: Key cards, PIN codes, and visitor logs.
Fences, gates, and guards.
Motion sensors, door sensors, and alarms.
Racks, cable trays, and ladders.
Fingerprint scanners, iris scanners, and facial recognition.

questions.explanation: The biometric layer includes fingerprint scanners, iris scanners, and facial recognition.

questions.question: How does a mantrap entry work?

choices.text: It is a small room with two doors; the first must close and lock before the second opens.
It is a single locked door opened only by a visitor escort.
It is a camera network recording every angle of the facility.
It is a biometric scanner that reads only fingerprints.
It is a firewall rule that blocks network tailgating.

questions.explanation: A mantrap controls entry with two doors so only one can be open after the first closes and locks.

questions.question: What does a mantrap help prevent?

choices.text: Overcooling caused by too many perforated tiles.
Tailgating, where someone sneaks in behind an authorized person.
Static electricity caused by low humidity.
Power loss during generator startup.
Asynchronous data replication over long distances.

questions.explanation: The guide define tailgating as someone sneaking in behind an authorized person.

questions.question: What is CCTV surveillance used for?

choices.text: Laser analysis of sampled smoke particles.
Battery backup while generators start.
Cameras recording every angle, 24/7.
Humidity management in server rooms.
Copying transaction logs between sites.

questions.explanation: CCTV is described as cameras recording every angle around the clock.

questions.question: Which sensors are listed for security alarms?

choices.text: Temperature sensors and humidity sensors only.
VESDA pipes and clean agent nozzles.
UPS batteries and generator coils.
Motion sensors and door sensors that trigger alerts.
Perforated tiles and solid tiles.

questions.explanation: Security alarms use motion sensors and door sensors to trigger alerts.

questions.question: How are non-employees handled under visitor management?

choices.text: Every non-employee can enter if they know the PUE value.
Every non-employee bypasses biometrics during maintenance.
Every non-employee receives unescorted rack access after signing in.
Every non-employee is treated as a network packet only.
Every non-employee is logged, escorted, and monitored.

questions.explanation: Visitor management requires logging, escorting, and monitoring every non-employee.

questions.question: What does NOC stand for in this context?

choices.text: Network Operations Center.
National Operations Code.
Network Overhead Controller.
Novec Operating Chamber.
Neutral Office Console.

questions.explanation: NOC is expanded as Network Operations Center.

questions.question: How does the guide say NOC is pronounced?

choices.text: N-O-C as separate letters only.
Knock.
Nuke.
Nock-er.
No-see.

questions.explanation: NOC is pronounced "knock".

questions.question: What is the NOC described as?

choices.text: The backup engine for generating electricity.
The climate rulebook for server rooms.
The command center of a data center.
The small two-door security room.
The clean agent fire suppression gas.

questions.explanation: The NOC is described as the command center.

questions.question: What room feature is often associated with the NOC?

choices.text: Raised-floor pedestals only.
Generator coils on the ceiling.
Water mist tanks in every rack.
Large screens on the walls.
Cold-site furniture waiting to be installed.

questions.explanation: The NOC is often a room with large wall screens.

questions.question: Which set of events would the NOC team see immediately?

choices.text: A visitor leaves the building, a chair moves, or a document is printed.
A tier number changes, a standard is published, or a site moves 300 km.
A generator is painted, a rack label changes, or an aisle gets swept.
A user sends email, a bank app opens, or a website displays a page.
A server goes down, a temperature sensor spikes, or a power unit fails.

questions.explanation: The guide give server down, temperature spike, and power unit failure as NOC visibility examples.

questions.question: Which power items are monitored?

choices.text: Utility power status, UPS battery levels, and generator readiness.
Door access logs, camera feeds, and intrusion alerts.
Temperature and humidity at every rack and HVAC health.
Connection speeds, outages, and traffic patterns.
Relative humidity, absolute humidity, and air quality.

questions.explanation: The monitoring table lists utility power status, UPS battery levels, and generator readiness for power.

questions.question: Which cooling items are monitored?

choices.text: Utility power status, UPS battery levels, and generator readiness.
Temperature and humidity at every rack, plus HVAC system health.
Connection speeds, outages, and traffic patterns.
Door access logs, camera feeds, and intrusion alerts.
Water leak detection under raised floors only.

questions.explanation: Cooling monitoring watches temperature and humidity at every rack and HVAC system health.

questions.question: Which network items are monitored?

choices.text: Utility status, UPS battery levels, and generator readiness.
Temperature and humidity at every rack.
Connection speeds, outages, and traffic patterns.
Door access logs and camera feeds.
Relative humidity, absolute humidity, and air quality.

questions.explanation: Network monitoring covers speeds, outages, and traffic patterns.

questions.question: Which security items are monitored?

choices.text: Connection speeds, outages, and traffic patterns.
UPS battery levels and generator readiness.
Temperature and humidity at every rack.
Door access logs, camera feeds, and intrusion alerts.
Raw data blocks and database transaction logs.

questions.explanation: The monitoring table lists access logs, camera feeds, and intrusion alerts for security.

questions.question: What leak issue is monitored?

choices.text: Diesel leaks inside database transaction logs.
Air leaks through access control PIN pads.
Smoke leaks through generator coils.
Network packet leaks through cold aisles.
Water leaks under raised floors from HVAC condensation.

questions.explanation: Leak monitoring checks for water under raised floors, such as from HVAC condensation.

questions.question: Which environmental conditions are monitored?

choices.text: Relative humidity, absolute humidity, and air quality.
Only uptime percentages and yearly downtime.
Only power usage and rack space.
Only cold, warm, and hot site readiness.
Only key cards, PIN codes, and guards.

questions.explanation: The environment row lists relative humidity, absolute humidity, and air quality.

questions.question: What does DCIM stand for?

choices.text: Data Cooling Information Monitor.
Data Center Infrastructure Management.
Disaster Control Incident Metric.
Direct Current Infrastructure Module.
Database Continuity Integration Method.

questions.explanation: DCIM is expanded as Data Center Infrastructure Management.

questions.question: What is the role of DCIM software?

choices.text: It replaces UPS batteries during the generator startup gap.
It seals hot or cold aisles to improve cooling efficiency.
It brings monitoring together into one dashboard and gives managers a complete picture.
It scans fingerprints, irises, and faces at sensitive areas.
It extinguishes fires without damaging electronics.

questions.explanation: DCIM consolidates infrastructure monitoring into a single dashboard for managers.

questions.question: Which items does DCIM track?

choices.text: Only visitor names, badges, and escort assignments.
Only Tier I through Tier IV annual downtime values.
Only ASHRAE recommended and allowable ranges.
Power usage, cooling efficiency, rack space, cable connections, and more.
Only cold site furniture and warm site unpacking status.

questions.explanation: The DCIM paragraph lists power usage, cooling efficiency, rack space, cable connections, and more.

questions.question: Why does disaster recovery exist?

choices.text: It exists only because planned maintenance causes 30% of downtime.
It exists to replace fire suppression systems in every rack.
It exists because normal offices have too many server racks.
It exists to improve PAC sensible heat ratio.
Disasters such as earthquakes, floods, fires, or major hardware failure can take down the primary data center.

questions.explanation: The guide list disasters and major hardware failure as reasons DR planning is needed.

questions.question: What is the core idea of disaster recovery?

choices.text: A second location, the DR site, can take over if the primary site fails.
A single UPS runs the primary site for weeks without generator power.
A mantrap prevents tailgating at the main entrance.
A CRAC unit changes into a CRAH during an outage.
A PUE value below 1.0 guarantees no disaster occurs.

questions.explanation: DR is based on having a second location that can take over after primary site failure.

questions.question: What does the guide call the main data center in DR context?

choices.text: The cold site, or CAC.
The primary site, or DC.
The hot aisle, or HAC.
The NOC, or command site.
The PDU, or final mile.

questions.explanation: The guide identify the main data center as the primary site or DC.

questions.question: What is the geographic separation trade-off for primary and DR sites?

choices.text: They must be as close as possible, even if the same flood can hit both.
They must be farther than 300 km for real-time replication to work.
They must be far enough to avoid the same disaster, but not so far that data transfer becomes too slow.
They must be inside the same building to reduce latency.
They must be separated only by different security layers.

questions.explanation: The DR section balances shared-disaster risk against network latency.

questions.question: What is the 40 to 50 km minimum DR distance intended to ensure?

choices.text: Protection against larger regional events such as severe storms and major flooding.
The maximum practical distance before latency is too high for real-time replication.
The ideal distance for zero data loss synchronous replication.
Different power grids and avoidance of localized events such as fires or industrial accidents.
The distance at which a cold site becomes a hot site.

questions.explanation: The table says 40 to 50 km minimum helps ensure different power grids and avoids localized events.

questions.question: What is the recommended DR site distance range?

choices.text: 40 to 50 km.
300 km maximum practical.
18 to 27 km.
5 to 30 km.

questions.explanation: The table lists 120 to 160 km as the recommended range.

questions.question: What does the 120 to 160 km recommended range protect against?

choices.text: Larger regional events such as severe storms and major flooding.
Only localized fires and industrial accidents.
Only power transfer switch delays.
Only static electricity in dry rooms.
Only visitor tailgating attempts.

questions.explanation: The recommended distance protects against regional events, not only localized ones.

questions.question: Why is 300 km described as the maximum practical DR distance?

choices.text: Beyond this, power grids are always identical.
Beyond this, network latency becomes too high for real-time data replication.
Beyond this, severe storms cannot affect either site.
Beyond this, all replication becomes synchronous with zero data loss.
Beyond this, a cold site automatically becomes cheaper.

questions.explanation: The table says distance beyond 300 km creates too much latency for real-time replication.

questions.question: What distance range does the guide call the usual sweet spot?

choices.text: 40 to 50 km.
5 to 30 km.
18 to 27 km.

questions.explanation: The trade-off note says the sweet spot is usually 120 to 160 km.

questions.question: Which statement matches a cold site?

choices.text: A building with some equipment that needs configuration, recovery in hours to days, medium cost.
A fully equipped mirror running and ready, recovery in minutes to hours, highest cost.
A data center with automatic fault tolerance and about 26 minutes of yearly downtime.
An empty building with power and cooling, no equipment installed, recovery in days to weeks, lowest cost.
A sealed cold aisle that delivers air only to server intakes.

questions.explanation: A cold site has power and cooling but no installed equipment; it is slowest and lowest cost.

questions.question: Which statement matches a warm site?

choices.text: An empty building with power and cooling but no equipment, recovery in days to weeks, lowest cost.
A fully equipped mirror ready to take over immediately, recovery in minutes to hours, highest cost.
A facility with one power path and no backup components.
A clean agent system that evaporates quickly and leaves no residue.
A building with some equipment that needs configuration and data loading, recovery in hours to days, medium cost.

questions.explanation: A warm site has some equipment but still needs configuration and data, with medium cost and recovery time.

questions.question: Which statement matches a hot site?

choices.text: A fully equipped mirror of the primary site, running and ready to take over, recovery in minutes to hours, highest cost.
An empty building with power and cooling but no installed equipment.
A building with some equipment that still needs data loading.
A site used only for cold air containment in server aisles.
A site that stores backup hard drives mailed nightly but has no equipment.

questions.explanation: A hot site is fully equipped and ready, so it has the fastest recovery and highest cost.

questions.question: In the DR analogy, what is a cold site compared to?

choices.text: A furnished apartment where you still need to unpack.
An empty apartment you would need to furnish.
A fully stocked second home with clothes already in the closet.
A house with two roads and automatic route switching.
A hospital with specialized systems for patients.

questions.explanation: The analogy compares a cold site to an empty apartment needing furniture.

questions.question: In the DR analogy, what is a warm site compared to?

choices.text: An empty apartment you would need to furnish.
A fully stocked second home with clothes already in the closet.
A furnished apartment where you still need to unpack your things.
A house with one road and a spare tire.
A window AC self-contained cooling unit.

questions.explanation: The analogy compares a warm site to a furnished apartment where you still need to unpack.

questions.question: In the DR analogy, what is a hot site compared to?

choices.text: An empty apartment you would need to furnish.
A furnished apartment where you still need to unpack.
A hospital room where a patient can survive at risky temperatures.
A fully stocked second home with your clothes already in the closet.
A house with one blocked road.

questions.explanation: The analogy maps a hot site to a fully stocked second home ready for use.

questions.question: What does RTO stand for and measure?

choices.text: Recovery Point Objective; the maximum acceptable data loss.
Real-Time Operation; whether replication happens synchronously.
Rack Temperature Objective; how warm a server intake may be.
Redundant Transfer Output; how quickly ATS switches power.
Recovery Time Objective; the maximum acceptable downtime or how long systems can be offline.

questions.explanation: RTO asks how long the organization can afford to be offline.

questions.question: If the RTO is 4 hours, what must happen?

choices.text: Systems must be restored within 4 hours of a disaster.
Data must be no more than 4 hours old at the DR site.
The UPS must run for exactly 4 hours before generators start.
The DR site must be exactly 4 km away.
The data center must lose exactly 4 hours of data.

questions.explanation: The guide give a 4-hour RTO as requiring restoration within 4 hours.

questions.question: What does RPO stand for and measure?

choices.text: Recovery Time Objective; the maximum acceptable downtime.
Recovery Point Objective; the maximum acceptable data loss measured in time.
Relative Power Output; the ratio of total facility power to IT power.
Rack Placement Objective; the distance between hot and cold aisles.
Remote Processing Order; the path from utility power to servers.

questions.explanation: RPO asks how much data loss is acceptable and is measured in time.

questions.question: If the RPO is 1 hour, what must be true?

choices.text: Systems must be fully restored within 1 hour, but no data age is implied.
The DR site must be 1 hour away by road.
There must be a copy of data no more than 1 hour old; work in the last hour might be lost.
UPS batteries must last exactly 1 hour.
The hot site must be 1 hour from the primary site.

questions.explanation: The guide explain that a 1-hour RPO means the backup copy must be at most 1 hour old.

questions.question: Which question does RTO answer in the table?

choices.text: How much data loss is acceptable?
How efficiently is power used?
How early is smoke detected?
How fast must we recover?
How much power does each rack use?

questions.explanation: The RTO table row asks how fast recovery must happen.

questions.question: Which question does RPO answer in the table?

choices.text: How fast must we recover?
How reliable is the tier?
How hot can the cold aisle be?
How many backup components exist?
How much data loss is acceptable?

questions.explanation: The RPO table row asks how much data loss is acceptable.

questions.question: Why does the DR site need data replication?

choices.text: It needs a copy of data to be useful during takeover.
It needs replication to keep the primary site colder.
It needs replication to reduce the number of guards.
It needs replication to replace power generators.
It needs replication only to lower the PUE score.

questions.explanation: The DR site needs a copy of data to be useful.

questions.question: Which statement describes offline replication?

choices.text: Data is written to both sites at exactly the same time.
Data is physically transported or transferred in scheduled batches, like mailing a backup drive nightly.
Data is copied over a network in real-time or near-real-time only.
Data is written to the primary first, then copied with a slight delay.
Raw data blocks are copied regardless of what is on them.

questions.explanation: Offline replication is batch transfer or physical transport, illustrated by mailing a backup hard drive nightly.

questions.question: What advantages are listed for offline replication?

choices.text: It provides zero data loss over short distances.
It works in real time over long distances with no delay.
It is simple, consistent, and low cost.
It restores systems in minutes to hours at highest cost.
It removes the need for a DR site copy of data.

questions.explanation: The advantages listed are simplicity, consistency, and low cost.

questions.question: What disadvantages are listed for offline replication?

choices.text: It cannot work unless sites are within 40 to 50 km.
It requires every write to complete at both sites simultaneously.
It is always the highest-cost replication option.
The DR data is always behind, and recovery can take days.
It prevents the DR site from having power and cooling.

questions.explanation: Offline replication lags behind and recovery may take days.

questions.question: Which statement describes online replication?

choices.text: Data is transported physically in scheduled batches only.
Data is stored only on removable drives mailed nightly.
Data is never copied until after a disaster occurs.
Data is copied only by visitor management staff.
Data is copied over a network connection in real-time or near-real-time.

questions.explanation: Online replication uses a network connection for real-time or near-real-time copying.

questions.question: What happens in synchronous replication?

choices.text: Data is written to both sites at the same time.
Data is written to the primary first and copied later with a delay.
Data is physically shipped on backup drives every night.
Only specific files are copied, never databases.
Raw storage blocks are copied only after recovery completes.

questions.explanation: Synchronous replication writes data to both locations simultaneously.

questions.question: What RPO characteristic is associated with synchronous replication?

choices.text: A small amount of recent data may be lost.
Zero data loss, with RPO approximately 0.
Data at the DR site is always behind by days.
RPO is unrelated to synchronous replication.
RPO equals the annual Tier IV downtime.

questions.explanation: Synchronous replication has zero data loss and RPO approximately 0.
