questions.question: What connection requirement limits synchronous replication?

choices.text: It requires long distances beyond 300 km to avoid latency.
It works best when data is mailed on physical drives.
It requires a fast, low-latency connection and works only over shorter distances.
It requires only a cold site with no equipment installed.
It requires fire suppression to use traditional sprinklers.

questions.explanation: Synchronous replication needs low latency, which limits practical distance.

questions.question: What happens in asynchronous replication?

choices.text: Data is written to both sites at the same time.
Data is never sent over a network connection.
Data is physically transported only in scheduled batches.
Data is written to the primary site first, then copied to the DR site with a slight delay.
Only clean agent configuration is copied.

questions.explanation: Asynchronous replication copies data after primary-site writes, with a slight delay.

questions.question: Which distance characteristic belongs to asynchronous replication?

choices.text: It only works over shorter distances with very low latency.
It requires sites to be in the same building.
It stops working beyond 40 to 50 km always.
It is unrelated to distance or latency.
It works over long distances.

questions.explanation: Asynchronous replication works over long distances.

questions.question: What data loss risk belongs to asynchronous replication?

choices.text: A small amount of recent data in transit during the delay could be lost.
Zero data loss because all writes complete at both sites simultaneously.
All data is always lost because there is no DR copy.
Only raw storage blocks can be lost, never database logs.
Data loss is impossible if the site is more than 300 km away.

questions.explanation: The delay means recent data still in transit could be lost.

questions.question: What does storage or block-level replication copy?

choices.text: Database transaction logs only.
Raw data blocks on the storage device.
Specific files or application data only.
Door access logs and camera feeds.
Temperature and humidity at each rack.

questions.explanation: The replication-level table says storage/block-level copies raw data blocks.

questions.question: What is storage/block-level replication best for?

choices.text: Keeping databases in sync by copying transaction logs.
Targeted replication of critical application files only.
Copying entire storage systems regardless of what is on them.
Monitoring power usage, cooling efficiency, and rack space.
Detecting smoke particles before visible flames.

questions.explanation: Block-level replication is best for copying entire storage systems independent of contents.

questions.question: What does database-level log-based replication copy?

choices.text: Raw data blocks on storage devices.
Specific application files only.
UPS battery levels.
Database transaction logs.
PUE calculation inputs only.

questions.explanation: The table says database-level log-based replication copies database transaction logs.

questions.question: What is database-level replication best for?

choices.text: Copying entire storage systems regardless of contents.
Targeted replication of individual application files.
Switching utility power to generator power.
Routing hot air back to cooling units.
Keeping databases in sync between sites.

questions.explanation: Database-level replication keeps databases synchronized between sites.

questions.question: What does application or file-level replication copy?

choices.text: Specific files or application data.
All raw data blocks from the storage device.
Only database transaction logs.
Only generator readiness information.
Only humidity readings from every rack.

questions.explanation: The table says application/file-level replication copies specific files or application data.

questions.question: What is application or file-level replication best for?

choices.text: Copying entire storage systems regardless of contents.
Targeted replication of critical application data.
Keeping databases in sync using transaction logs.
Maintaining positive air pressure to keep dust out.
Preventing tailgating through a two-door room.

questions.explanation: Application/file-level replication is best for targeted replication of critical application data.
